title: Why Greenhouse Gases Trap Heat in the Atmosphere

lesson.learn:
Greenhouse gases trap heat because they absorb infrared radiation leaving Earth’s surface and re-emit some of that energy back toward the ground and lower atmosphere. Without that process, Earth would be much colder and far less hospitable. The greenhouse effect is natural and essential; the problem is that human activity is increasing the amount of heat-trapping gas in the air.

Carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, and water vapor all play roles in the energy balance. The physics is simple at its core: more greenhouse gas means more energy kept in the climate system.
lesson.simpler: Greenhouse gases work like a thermal blanket. They let sunlight in, but they slow the escape of heat back out to space.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Greenhouse gases trap heat because they absorb and re-radiate infrared energy.
lesson.deeperDive:
Solar energy mostly arrives as visible light. Earth warms and then gives that energy back off as infrared radiation. Molecules such as carbon dioxide and methane interact strongly with those infrared wavelengths, which shifts the planet’s energy balance. That is why even small changes in greenhouse gas concentration can matter globally.

Climate feedbacks can amplify the effect. More warming can increase water vapor and melt reflective ice, which in turn increases heat absorption. Understanding this process is essential for climate science, because it connects emissions, temperature rise, and the stability of future climate.
lesson.funFact: The greenhouse effect is natural. The modern problem is that it is getting stronger.

people: Svante Arrhenius - early scientist to connect CO2 and warming
John Tyndall - studied infrared absorption by gases
Syukuro Manabe - advanced climate modeling of greenhouse effects
Climate researchers - teams measuring atmospheric change
places: Earth’s atmosphere - the main system being warmed
NASA and NOAA labs - climate observation centers
Industrial regions - places where emissions are measured
Polar regions - where feedbacks are especially strong
years: 1859 - Tyndall demonstrates gas absorption of heat
20th century - greenhouse physics becomes central to climate science
21st century - emissions growth makes the effect more visible
concepts: Infrared radiation - heat energy emitted by warm objects
Energy balance - the incoming and outgoing energy of Earth
Radiative forcing - a change that alters climate energy flow
Feedback loop - a process that amplifies change
Atmospheric composition - the mix of gases in air
